target.name: KoBold Metals
target.founder: Kurt House, Josh Goldman, Jeff Jurinak

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $2.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 9 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $120M - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
KoBolt Metals is a Series C-stage climate and energy company that has raised over $400M. The valuation analysis triangulates across a peer set of nine same-sector, same/adjacent-stage companies, comprising eight private firms and one public comparable (LNZA). Key private peers include Electric Hydrogen ($1.0B, Series C), Amogy ($700M, Series C), Commonwealth Fusion Systems ($5.0B, Series B), and Redwood Materials ($6.0B, Series D), with peer valuations ranging from $120M to $6.0B.

Two primary methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C advancement to the company's last post-money valuation of $2.0B, implying $3.4B. Peer-median analysis across the nine-company comparable set yields a median valuation of $500M, reflecting wide dispersion within the climate tech sector. The peer median sits notably below the company's stated current valuation, suggesting either premium positioning relative to peers or stage/commercial maturity assumptions embedded in the last round.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $425M (low) to $3.9B (high), with a median point estimate of $1.9B. This wide range reflects material uncertainty around commercialization timelines and technology-to-market risk typical of frontier-energy ventures. The $2.0B current valuation sits near the median, though reconciliation of the peer-median method requires clarity on whether comparable companies operate at different development or revenue stages.
